question: Will the two leaders reach a settlement within 60 days?

mediator.description: A neutral third party facilitating the settlement negotiation

settlement_proposal.description: A formal written settlement proposal under consideration

campaign_cost_report.description: Periodic assessment of financial and operational costs from the contested campaign

leader_has_framework.description: Whether a given leader has accepted the existing framework as a basis for negotiation

settlement_signed_by_leader.description: Record that a specific leader has signed a particular settlement proposal

campaign_cost_rising.description: Indicates that the cost of the contested campaign is increasing for a given leader

negotiation_session.description: A mediated negotiation session involving both leaders and the mediator

proposal_review_assignment.description: Assignment of a settlement proposal to a leader for review and decision

framework_acceptance_event.description: A leader formally accepts the existing negotiation framework

settlement_proposal_drafted_event.description: A settlement proposal is drafted by a party or mediator

settlement_signing_event.description: A leader signs a settlement proposal, making it binding for that party

cost_report_issued_event.description: A campaign cost report is issued, showing rising costs

mediation_session_held_event.description: A scheduled mediation session takes place

unmodeled_actor_action.description: schema-scoped scaffolding: an actor action whose semantics could not be compiled; exact content preserved; counted as a fallback

mediated_settlement_process.description: State machine for the two-party mediated settlement process

transitions.guard: both leaders have signed the same proposal_id
review_deadline passed without signing by either leader

settlement_decision_body.description: The authority structure for ratifying a settlement between leader_a and leader_b
settlement_decision_body.evidence: No alternative decision mechanism described; settlement requires both leaders' consent by definition.

signing_deadline_constraint.description: A settlement must be signed by both leaders no later than 2024-01-13 23:59:59 to be considered within the 60-day horizon
signing_deadline_constraint.rule: If settlement_signed_by_leader.signature_date > '2024-01-13T23:59:59Z' for any leader, the settlement is not valid within the prediction horizon

negotiation_framework_document.description: The existing framework that both sides have acknowledged as a starting point
negotiation_framework_document.usage_constraint: Must be referenced in any settlement proposal

campaign_budget.description: Financial resources being consumed by the contested campaign
campaign_budget.usage_constraint: Rising costs act as pressure to settle

cost_publication_rule.description: Campaign cost reports are published and observable by both leaders and the mediator
cost_publication_rule.rule: Any campaign_cost_report with field cost_trend='rising' is visible to all actors immediately upon issuance

framework_acceptance_visibility.description: Each leader's acceptance of the framework is known to the other leader and the mediator
framework_acceptance_visibility.rule: leader_has_framework fact is public to all negotiation participants

leader_a.role: Party to the dispute, decision-maker for signing settlement
leader_a.why_consequential: Directly holds one of the two required signatures for settlement
affordances: Accept or reject the negotiation framework
Sign or refuse to sign a settlement proposal

leader_b.role: Party to the dispute, decision-maker for signing settlement
leader_b.why_consequential: Directly holds the other required signature; settlement impossible without their consent
affordances: Accept or reject the negotiation framework
Sign or refuse to sign a settlement proposal

mediator.why_consequential: Controls agenda, drafting of proposals, and timing of sessions; may influence both leaders
affordances: Present cost reports to both sides

outcome_predicates.option_true: Both leaders have signed the same proposal_id with signature_date <= 2024-01-13T23:59:59Z
outcome_predicates.option_false: Either no settlement exists, or at least one leader has not signed, or signing date exceeds the horizon

unresolved_mechanisms: The exact process for how the mediator selects which proposal to prioritize is not specified; assumed to be discretionary.
The mechanism by which 'rising costs' translate into concrete pressure (e.g., forced mediation schedule) is not modeled.
Whether either leader has veto power over specific proposal terms is not defined; assumed to be inherent in unanimous signing requirement.
assumptions: Both leaders have de facto authority to bind their respective parties to a settlement without additional ratification (assumed from evidence 'two-party mediated settlement').
The existing framework is materially complete enough to generate a specific proposal within the horizon.
No external events (e.g., military escalation, third-party intervention) will disrupt the negotiation process within the 60 days.
The mediator remains neutral and actively engaged throughout the period.

auto_repairs: institution 'settlement_decision_body' aggregation defaulted to majority (none declared; labeled assumed)

missing_decisive_elements: No representation of the actual disputed stakes (territory, resources, legal status) that drive willingness to settle
No model of domestic political pressure (e.g., cabinet opposition, public opinion polls, election cycles) that can override leader consent
No mechanism for external coercive actions (sanctions, military escalation, trade embargoes) that could force or prevent signing
No timeline constraints on framework acceptance — leaders could accept framework on day 1 or day 59, making the 60-day horizon meaningless
No concept of proposal rejection or counter-proposal; only 'accept' or 'fail' binary
No model of trust erosion or personal animosity between leaders that can block even mutually beneficial deals
relabeled_progress_bars: The 'settlement_signed_by_one' state appears as progress but is misleading: one signature does not reduce the time needed for the second, nor does it guarantee the second will ever come
The 'proposal_under_review' state is a black box with no temporal bounds — review could take 58 days then fail, making the entire process path a false indicator
hidden_answer_risks: The model assumes that if both leaders have accepted the framework (a boolean), they will eventually proceed to a proposal — but framework acceptance could be purely performative, with no intent to sign
The 'campaign_cost_rising' fact type exists but is never wired into any transition guard or probability model — it is decorative, not causal, creating a false sense of mechanistic cost pressure
No upper limit on how many mediation sessions can occur; leaders could endlessly attend sessions without moving toward signing, while the model has no 'stall' state
direct_human_reaction_encodings: No encoding of emotional responses (anger, humiliation, betrayal) that could cause a leader to refuse signing even when costs rise
No encoding of face-saving behavior: leaders may sign only after symbolic concessions that the model cannot represent (e.g., changing 'text' field of proposal)
No encoding of last-minute brinkmanship: a leader may deliberately delay signature until the final hours, which the 60-day deadline treats as equivalent to early signing
public_posture_vs_private_reality_confla: The model treats 'settlement_signed_by_leader' as a binary public fact (is_valid: bool), but leaders may sign secretly or under duress, with private reservations that make the signature revocable or unenforceable
The 'leader_has_framework' fact conflates public announcement with genuine acceptance — no distinction between 'stated yes' and 'internally committed'
Campaign_cost_report is issued by the leaders themselves, but leaders could fabricate or suppress cost data to manipulate the negotiation — the model has no integrity check
missing_nonhuman_mechanisms: No model of media coverage or leaks that could accelerate or derail settlement
No model of international financial markets or credit rating changes that create exogenous pressure
No model of natural disasters or pandemics that could reset priorities (e.g., both leaders shift focus away from settlement)
No model of physical security — an assassination, coup, or health emergency could remove a leader mid-process
No model of technical delays (document translation, legal review, printing, travel) that could push signing past 60 days even if both agree
